metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity UI beats; exhibits keep their own look (MUSINIQUE.md graded-exhibit law).
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Spanish (one-word budget — Musinique persona). Wagwan stays Bear's.
metadata.charter: MUSINIQUE.md — fights for the indie musician; receipts law; roll-your-own law; no fake numbers
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW: UI at B00 (with RESULT lines) / verdict / handoff / outro only; each inner beat carries an illustration spec in props.illustration (kind: concept | pattern | code | exhibit) — build per medhavy_com/youtube/mcp/remotion-src worked example + skills/animated-deck patterns. ROLL-YOUR-OWN LAW: the handoff paste is a deck piece the viewer keeps. RECEIPTS LAW: claims trace to RECEIPTS.md beside the playlist; illustrative examples labeled as such.

beats.narration_text: The sorting rule — how often does this task need my judgment — finally made the three lanes click for me: every step is chat, once-then-repeats is Co-work, never-at-runtime is Claude Code. Help me apply it to my own week: (1) sort my five recurring music tasks into the right lane using that one question; (2) flag which of them I'm currently jamming into chat and retyping every week; (3) remind me where the 'none of these can hear' limit bites, so I never hand a mix critique to a tool judging my description instead of the sound.

props.command: The sorting rule — how often does this task need my judgment — finally made the three lanes click for me: every step is chat, once-then-repeats is Co-work, never-at-runtime is Claude Code. Help me apply it to my own week: (1) sort my five recurring music tasks into the right lane using that one question; (2) flag which of them I'm currently jamming into chat and retyping every week; (3) remind me where the 'none of these can hear' limit bites, so I never hand a mix critique to a tool judging my description instead of the sound.
output: chat — judgment, with you in the loop
Claude Code — the pipeline you own

beats.narration_text: Lane one: Claude chat. A thinking partner — you're in the loop for every step. Lyrics, arrangement critique, the hard calls. Lane two: Co-work. A background worker — you hand it a folder and a recipe, it runs while you rehearse. Lane three: Claude Code. An engineering room, for the musician building a machine.

The sorting rule is one question: how often does this task need YOUR judgment? Every step — that's chat. Once, up front, then it repeats — that's Co-work. Never at runtime, because you froze the judgment into the system — that's Claude Code.

The mistake I keep watching musicians make: pipeline-shaped work jammed into chat, retyped every week like it's new. Or worse — creative judgment handed to a scheduler. The tool didn't fail you. The sorting did.

One honest limit before we go further, and I need you to hold it: none of these tools can hear. 'Analyze my mix' always means 'reason about my description of my mix.' Anyone selling you AI that listens from a chat window is selling you the description, not the ear.

Here's the season's deal, plainly. Six more episodes. Each one ends with a prompt you paste and keep — a digest, a source filter, a voice file, a catalog audit, a pitch machine, a review gate. Follow the whole season and you own a promotion deck you were about to rent from a stranger.

Let's recap with Claude. One page. Chat thinks with you. Co-work runs your recipes. Code builds your machine. None of them hear. All of them, pointed right, work for you — the same way the platforms' machines work against you.

props.artifactTitle: Claude, In Your Corner — one page
artifactLines: Chat: synchronous judgment — you're in the loop every step (lyrics, critique, the hard calls).
Co-work: asynchronous recipes — folder + instructions + schedule; it runs while you rehearse.
Claude Code: the pipeline room — skills, gates, and systems you own and re-run.
The limit, stated: none of them can hear audio. And the deck you build this season is yours — that's the point.

beats.narration_text: Your turn — and this one costs nothing. Paste this with your real week in it. What comes back is your map for the rest of the season: which lane each piece of your grind actually belongs in.

props.command: Sort my music work into three lanes: (1) tasks needing my judgment every step, (2) recipes I repeat weekly that could run on a schedule, (3) systems worth building once and re-running. Here's my current weekly workload: [describe your week].
